title: SEGMENT 15 — Methylation in cancer and the liquid biopsy revolution

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Methylation in cancer and the liquid biopsy revolution
Now let's bring all the methodology back to one of its biggest real-world applications: cancer.
The methylation patterns of cancer cells are profoundly disrupted, and the disruption has a characteristic signature that we touched on in Segment 3.
Let me unpack the cancer methylome carefully, because it's the basis for some of the most exciting clinical methylation tests on the market today.
The canonical cancer methylation signature has two parts.
Global hypomethylation.
The bulk of the genome — particularly the repetitive elements, the intergenic regions, the partially methylated domains — loses methylation in cancer.
This contributes to genomic instability, retrotransposon reactivation, and the broad chromosomal chaos characteristic of tumor cells.
Focal hypermethylation at CpG island promoters.
A specific set of CpG island promoters, often hundreds to thousands per tumor, become aberrantly methylated, silencing the associated genes.
Many of these silenced genes are tumor suppressors, DNA repair genes, or cell-cycle regulators.
The combined pattern — global loss plus focal gain — is so consistent across cancer types that methylation alone can often classify a tumor's tissue of origin with high accuracy.
The cell-type-specificity of methylation is the key to the liquid biopsy revolution.
Every cell type in the body has its own characteristic methylation pattern — its own combination of methylated and unmethylated CpGs across its genome.
When a cell dies, it releases its DNA into the bloodstream as cell-free DNA — short fragments, typically 150 to 170 base pairs long, circulating in plasma.
A healthy person has cell-free DNA in their blood coming mostly from blood cells dying as part of normal turnover, with smaller contributions from other tissues.

A cancer patient has additional cell-free DNA coming from dying tumor cells — sometimes a substantial fraction, particularly in advanced disease, but typically a small minority (1 percent or less) in early-stage cancer.
If you can read the methylation patterns of cell-free DNA in plasma, you can in principle do two things.
First, detect that some fraction of the DNA has tumor-like methylation patterns.

A blood sample that contains DNA fragments with the methylation signature of, say, hepatocellular carcinoma — fragments that look methylated in places where normal liver isn't, and unmethylated where normal liver is — provides evidence of a liver tumor.
Second, infer the tissue of origin of the tumor.
Different cancers — colorectal, lung, breast, pancreatic, ovarian — have characteristic methylation signatures that reflect both the originating cell type and the cancer-specific methylation changes.
Methylation-based classifiers trained on hundreds of cancer types can read the cell-free DNA methylation pattern and predict not just "cancer present" but "cancer from tissue X.
The flagship commercial product in this space is Galleri, made by the company GRAIL, launched commercially in 2021.
Galleri is a blood test that measures methylation patterns at over 100,000 selected genomic regions in cell-free DNA, using a targeted methylation sequencing approach.
The test claims to detect a signal consistent with cancer for over 50 different cancer types, many of which currently have no screening tests at all.
When the test detects a cancer signal, it also predicts the tissue of origin, narrowing the diagnostic workup that follows.
Here is the analogy.
The Galleri test is methylation-based liquid biopsy — it detects roughly 50 cancer types from cell-free DNA methylation patterns in a single blood draw.
The methylation patterns of cancer cells leak into the plasma as fragmented DNA; the test reads those patterns and infers both presence of cancer and likely tissue of origin.
It's a profound application of the same chemistry and analysis we've been building up across this lecture.

There are similar tests in development from multiple companies — Guardant has a methylation-based test, Exact Sciences (the maker of Cologuard) uses methylation as part of its colorectal cancer screen, and several other companies have methylation-based monitoring tests for cancer recurrence after treatment.

The clinical validation of these tests is still in progress — large randomized trials are running to assess whether early methylation-based detection actually improves survival outcomes — but the technology has moved from research curiosity to commercial reality in the span of about a decade.
Methylation has also become central to methylation-based tumor classification for diagnostic pathology.

The DKFZ in Heidelberg developed a methylation classifier for brain tumors that has become the gold standard for diagnosing CNS tumors — it can distinguish over 90 distinct brain tumor subtypes from a single methylation array on a tumor biopsy.
Similar classifiers exist for sarcomas, pediatric tumors, and other diagnostic challenges.
In many cancer centers today, the methylation profile is a routine part of tumor characterization, alongside histology and mutation testing.
The cancer methylome is also a guide to treatment.

Some chemotherapy drugs — particularly the so-called epigenetic drugs like azacitidine and decitabine — work by inhibiting DNA methyltransferases, broadly demethylating the genome and reactivating silenced tumor suppressors.
These drugs are approved for treating certain myelodysplastic syndromes and leukemias.
Methylation status of specific genes — MGMT in glioblastoma, for example — predicts response to alkylating chemotherapy and is now part of standard clinical practice.
The translation from methylation biology to methylation-guided therapy is still early, but the foundation is there.
